era_context: The year 1945 was the climactic final year of World War II, the deadliest conflict in human history, which had raged since 1939 in Europe and 1937 in Asia. The Allied powers — the United States, Soviet Union, United Kingdom, China, and France among others — converged on the Axis powers from all directions. The year saw the collapse of Nazi Germany, the atomic bombings of Japan, the founding of the United Nations, and the onset of the geopolitical conditions that would define the Cold War. Decolonisation accelerated as European empires, exhausted by war, began losing their grip on colonial territories in Asia and the Middle East. The Holocaust reached its conclusion as Allied forces liberated concentration and extermination camps, revealing the full scale of Nazi genocide.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa outside colonial administrative centres
Indigenous communities in the Amazon basin and interior Australia
Central Asian populations under Soviet control
Pacific Island societies disrupted by military operations but poorly documented outside combat records

events.title: Yalta Conference shapes post-war order
events.description: From 4–11 February 1945, Franklin Roosevelt, Winston Churchill, and Joseph Stalin met at Yalta in Crimea to negotiate the post-war reorganisation of Europe and the Pacific. Key agreements included the division of Germany into occupation zones, the promise of free elections in liberated Eastern Europe (a pledge Stalin would systematically violate), Soviet entry into the war against Japan within three months of Germany's surrender, and the framework for the United Nations including the Security Council veto. The conference also secretly ceded territorial concessions to the Soviet Union in exchange for its Pacific War commitment, including southern Sakhalin and the Kuril Islands. Yalta became deeply controversial in the West as Soviet domination of Eastern Europe became apparent.

sources.name: Foreign Relations of the United States: The Conferences at Malta and Yalta, 1945 (U.S. Department of State)
S.M. Plokhy, Yalta: The Price of Peace (Viking, 2010)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through official minutes, memoranda, and participant memoirs. Some side agreements remained classified until later declassification.

events.title: Liberation of Auschwitz and revelation of the Holocaust
events.description: On 27 January 1945, soldiers of the Soviet 322nd Rifle Division of the 60th Army entered the Auschwitz-Birkenau extermination camp complex in southern Poland, liberating approximately 7,000 surviving prisoners. The SS had forced roughly 60,000 prisoners on death marches westward in preceding days. Throughout 1945, Allied forces liberated dozens of Nazi concentration and extermination camps — including Bergen-Belsen (April 15), Buchenwald (April 11), Dachau (April 29), and Mauthausen (May 5) — progressively revealing the systematic murder of approximately six million Jews and millions of Roma, Slavs, disabled persons, political prisoners, and others. The liberation of the camps provided irrefutable physical evidence of industrial genocide and profoundly shaped post-war consciousness, international law, and the impetus for human rights frameworks.
key_figures: Anatoly Shapiro (Soviet officer, first unit to enter Auschwitz main camp)
Rudolf Höss (Auschwitz commandant, later tried and executed)

sources.name: Soviet military reports from the 60th Army, January 1945
Raul Hilberg, The Destruction of the European Jews (Yale University Press, 3rd ed., 2003)
Auschwitz-Birkenau State Museum archives and physical site evidence

events.certainty_note: Confirmed by massive documentary, physical, photographic, and testimonial evidence from all sides. Nazi administrative records, survivor testimony, and Allied military documentation are mutually corroborating.

events.title: Battle of Iwo Jima
events.description: From 19 February to 26 March 1945, U.S. Marines fought one of the bloodiest battles of the Pacific War to capture the small volcanic island of Iwo Jima from its heavily entrenched Japanese garrison. The Japanese commander, General Kuribayashi, had constructed an elaborate network of tunnels and bunkers designed to inflict maximum casualties. Of approximately 21,000 Japanese defenders, fewer than 300 survived. American casualties were approximately 6,800 killed and 19,200 wounded. The island's airfields were strategically important for fighter escort of B-29 bombing raids on Japan and as emergency landing strips. The iconic photograph of Marines raising the flag on Mount Suribachi became one of the most reproduced images of the war.
key_figures: Holland M. Smith (U.S. Marine commanding general)

sources.name: U.S. Marine Corps Historical Division, Iwo Jima: Amphibious Epic (1954)
James Bradley, Flags of Our Fathers (Bantam, 2000)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by military records, contemporary reporting, and photographic evidence.

events.title: Firebombing of Tokyo
events.description: On the night of 9–10 March 1945, 334 B-29 Superfortress bombers of the U.S. XXI Bomber Command conducted a low-altitude incendiary raid on Tokyo, code-named Operation Meetinghouse. The raid created a massive firestorm that destroyed approximately 41 square kilometres of the city and killed an estimated 80,000–100,000 civilians, making it the single deadliest air raid in history — more lethal than either atomic bombing. Over subsequent months, the U.S. strategic bombing campaign systematically fire-bombed dozens of Japanese cities, destroying vast urban areas and killing hundreds of thousands. The campaign raised profound moral questions about area bombing of civilian populations that remain debated by historians and ethicists.
key_figures: Henry H. Arnold (U.S. Army Air Forces chief)

sources.name: United States Strategic Bombing Survey: The Effects of Strategic Bombing on Japan's War Economy (1946)
Kenneth P. Werrell, Blankets of Fire: U.S. Bombers over Japan during World War II (Smithsonian, 1996)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by U.S. military records and Japanese government surveys. Casualty figures have a range due to wartime disruption of Japanese civil records, but the scale is not in dispute.

events.title: Battle of Okinawa
events.description: From 1 April to 22 June 1945, American forces fought the last and largest amphibious battle of the Pacific War on the island of Okinawa. The Japanese garrison of approximately 100,000 troops, commanded by General Ushijima, fought a determined defence in depth. Japanese forces also launched mass kamikaze attacks against the Allied fleet, sinking or damaging hundreds of ships. The battle resulted in approximately 12,500 American dead, over 100,000 Japanese military dead (including Ushijima's suicide), and an estimated 40,000–150,000 Okinawan civilian deaths — the civilian population suffered terribly from crossfire, Japanese coercion into mass suicide, and displacement. The staggering casualties on all sides heavily influenced American decision-making regarding the use of atomic weapons to avoid an invasion of the Japanese home islands.
key_figures: Simon Bolivar Buckner Jr. (U.S. Tenth Army commander, killed in action)

sources.name: U.S. Army Center of Military History, Okinawa: The Last Battle (1948)
George Feifer, The Battle of Okinawa: The Blood and the Bomb (Lyons Press, 2001)
Okinawa Prefectural Peace Memorial Museum archives and oral history collections

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented. Okinawan civilian casualty figures remain debated due to incomplete wartime records and political sensitivities in Japan regarding military-compelled civilian suicides.

events.title: Fall of Berlin and suicide of Adolf Hitler
events.description: The Battle of Berlin (16 April–2 May 1945) was the final major offensive of the European theatre. The Soviet forces under Marshals Zhukov and Konev launched a massive assault with 2.5 million troops against the German capital, defended by a mixture of Wehrmacht, Waffen-SS, Volkssturm militia, and Hitler Youth. Fierce urban combat resulted in enormous casualties — Soviet forces suffered approximately 80,000 killed; German military and civilian deaths numbered in the hundreds of thousands. On 30 April, Adolf Hitler committed suicide in his bunker beneath the Reich Chancellery. The Berlin garrison surrendered on 2 May. The fall of Berlin effectively ended the Nazi regime and the war in Europe, though localised fighting continued until the formal surrender.
key_figures: Helmuth Weidling (Berlin defence commandant, surrendered the garrison)

sources.name: Antony Beevor, The Fall of Berlin 1945 (Viking, 2002)
Soviet General Staff operational records, translated in David Glantz & Jonathan House, When Titans Clashed (University Press of Kansas, 1995)
Hugh Trevor-Roper, The Last Days of Hitler (Macmillan, 1947) — based on contemporary British intelligence investigation

events.certainty_note: Massively documented. Hitler's death was confirmed by Soviet forensic evidence and multiple witness testimonies, though the Soviets initially spread disinformation about it.

events.title: Unconditional surrender of Nazi Germany (V-E Day)
events.description: On 7 May 1945, General Alfred Jodl signed the unconditional surrender of all German forces at SHAEF headquarters in Reims, France. At Soviet insistence, a second signing ceremony was held in Berlin on 8–9 May, with Field Marshal Wilhelm Keitel signing before Marshal Zhukov. Victory in Europe Day (V-E Day) was celebrated on 8 May across the Western Allied nations and on 9 May in the Soviet Union. The surrender ended nearly six years of war in Europe that had killed an estimated 35–40 million Europeans, devastated the continent's infrastructure, displaced tens of millions, and irrevocably altered the global balance of power. The occupation and division of Germany into four Allied zones began immediately.
key_figures: Alfred Jodl (signed German surrender at Reims)
Dwight D. Eisenhower (Supreme Allied Commander Europe)
Georgy Zhukov (Soviet representative at Berlin ceremony)
Karl Dönitz (Hitler's designated successor as head of state)

sources.name: German Instrument of Surrender, 7 May 1945 (Reims) and 8 May 1945 (Berlin)
Foreign Relations of the United States, 1945, Vol. III: European Advisory Commission; Austria; Germany

events.certainty_note: The instrument of surrender is a surviving primary document. The event is among the most thoroughly documented in modern history.

events.title: United Nations Charter signed in San Francisco
events.description: On 26 June 1945, delegates from 50 nations signed the United Nations Charter at the conclusion of the United Nations Conference on International Organization, which had convened on 25 April. The Charter established the United Nations as the successor to the failed League of Nations, with a General Assembly, a Security Council with five permanent veto-wielding members (the United States, Soviet Union, United Kingdom, France, and China), an International Court of Justice, and a Secretariat. The Charter entered into force on 24 October 1945 after ratification by the five permanent members and a majority of signatories. The UN became the central institution of post-war international governance, though the veto power built superpower gridlock into its architecture from the outset.
key_figures: Harry S. Truman (U.S. President, addressed the closing session)
Edward Stettinius Jr. (U.S. Secretary of State, conference chair)
Jan Smuts (South African Prime Minister, wrote the preamble)

sources.name: Charter of the United Nations, signed 26 June 1945, San Francisco
Documents of the United Nations Conference on International Organization, San Francisco, 1945 (22 volumes)
Stephen C. Schlesinger, Act of Creation: The Founding of the United Nations (Westview, 2003)

events.certainty_note: Primary documentary record is extensive and fully preserved.

events.title: Potsdam Conference and ultimatum to Japan
events.description: From 17 July to 2 August 1945, the last wartime summit of the major Allied leaders took place at Potsdam near Berlin. Truman (replacing the deceased Roosevelt), Stalin, and Churchill (replaced mid-conference by Clement Attlee after the Labour election victory) discussed the occupation of Germany, reparations, the redrawing of European borders — including the Oder-Neisse line establishing Poland's western boundary at Germany's expense — and the war against Japan. The Potsdam Declaration of 26 July demanded Japan's unconditional surrender, warning of 'prompt and utter destruction' without specifically mentioning atomic weapons. Japan's government, under Prime Minister Suzuki, initially chose to 'mokusatsu' (ignore or treat with silent contempt) the declaration, a response the Allies interpreted as rejection.

sources.name: Foreign Relations of the United States: The Conference of Berlin (The Potsdam Conference), 1945 (2 volumes)
Potsdam Declaration, 26 July 1945

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented through official minutes, communiqués, and participant diaries. Truman's diary entries from Potsdam confirming his knowledge of the successful Trinity test are extant.

events.title: Trinity test — first nuclear weapon detonated
events.description: On 16 July 1945, the United States detonated the world's first nuclear device at the Trinity test site in the Jornada del Muerto desert of New Mexico. The plutonium implosion device, codenamed 'Gadget,' produced an explosion equivalent to approximately 21 kilotons of TNT, vaporising the steel test tower and creating a crater of radioactive glass (trinitite). The test validated the implosion design developed by the Manhattan Project under J. Robert Oppenheimer's scientific direction. The successful test gave President Truman leverage at the ongoing Potsdam Conference and set the stage for the atomic bombings of Japan the following month. The Trinity test inaugurated the nuclear age, fundamentally transforming warfare, geopolitics, and the existential condition of human civilisation.
key_figures: J. Robert Oppenheimer (Manhattan Project scientific director)

sources.name: Manhattan Engineer District records, National Archives (RG 77)
Richard Rhodes, The Making of the Atomic Bomb (Simon & Schuster, 1986)

events.certainty_note: Documented by extensive scientific instrumentation, photographs, film, and participant accounts. The test site remains physically extant.

events.title: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
events.description: On 6 August 1945, a U.S. B-29 bomber named Enola Gay dropped a uranium gun-type atomic bomb ('Little Boy') on Hiroshima, killing an estimated 70,000–80,000 people instantly and ultimately causing approximately 140,000 deaths by the end of 1945. On 9 August, a plutonium implosion bomb ('Fat Man') was dropped on Nagasaki, killing approximately 40,000 people immediately and an estimated 70,000 by year's end. The bombings destroyed both cities' centres and inflicted severe radiation injuries on survivors (hibakusha). These remain the only use of nuclear weapons in warfare. The bombings, combined with the Soviet declaration of war on 8 August, precipitated Japan's decision to surrender, though debate continues among historians about the relative weight of each factor and whether the bombings were militarily necessary.
key_figures: Emperor Hirohito (intervened to accept surrender terms)

sources.name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum archives and survivor testimonies
United States Strategic Bombing Survey: The Effects of Atomic Bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1946)
J. Samuel Walker, Prompt and Utter Destruction: Truman and the Use of Atomic Bombs Against Japan (University of North Carolina Press, 3rd ed., 2016)

events.certainty_note: Among the most documented events in history. Casualty figures are approximate due to wartime disruption and the delayed effects of radiation, but the broad ranges are well established.

events.title: Japan surrenders, ending World War II (V-J Day)
events.description: On 15 August 1945, Emperor Hirohito broadcast the Gyokuon-hōsō (Jewel Voice Broadcast) to the Japanese public, announcing Japan's acceptance of the Allied surrender terms. This followed intense internal debate within the Japanese Supreme War Council, where a faction of military hardliners opposed surrender even after the atomic bombings and Soviet invasion. Hirohito's unprecedented personal intervention broke the deadlock. The formal instrument of surrender was signed on 2 September 1945 aboard the USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay, with Foreign Minister Shigemitsu and General Umezu signing for Japan. The ceremony, presided over by General MacArthur as Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers, ended the deadliest conflict in human history, which had killed an estimated 70–85 million people worldwide.
key_figures: Emperor Hirohito (made the decision to surrender)
Mamoru Shigemitsu (signed for the Japanese government)
Yoshijirō Umezu (signed for the Japanese military)
Chester Nimitz (signed for the United States)

sources.name: Japanese Instrument of Surrender, 2 September 1945
Gyokuon-hōsō (Imperial Rescript on Surrender), 15 August 1945 — radio broadcast recording extant
Herbert P. Bix, Hirohito and the Making of Modern Japan (HarperCollins, 2000)

events.certainty_note: Documented by the surviving instrument of surrender, broadcast recordings, military records, and extensive photography and film.

events.title: Soviet Union declares war on Japan and invades Manchuria
events.description: On 8 August 1945, the Soviet Union declared war on Japan, exactly three months after Germany's surrender as agreed at Yalta. On 9 August, 1.5 million Soviet troops under Marshal Vasilevsky launched a massive three-front invasion of Japanese-occupied Manchuria (Operation August Storm). The Kwantung Army, once Japan's most powerful field force but heavily depleted by transfers to other fronts, was overwhelmed within days. Soviet forces also invaded southern Sakhalin and the Kuril Islands, and advanced into northern Korea. The campaign was one of the most decisive operational victories in military history, destroying Japanese power on the Asian mainland in roughly two weeks. The Soviet occupation of northern Korea established the conditions for the subsequent division of the peninsula.

sources.name: David Glantz, The Soviet Strategic Offensive in Manchuria, 1945: August Storm (Frank Cass, 2003)
Soviet General Staff operational studies, declassified post-1991

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through Soviet military records and Japanese post-war accounts. The exact role of Soviet entry versus the atomic bombs in precipitating Japanese surrender remains actively debated.

events.title: Soviet domination of Eastern Europe solidifies
events.description: Throughout 1945, the Soviet Union systematically established political control over the countries its armies had occupied in the course of defeating Germany. In Poland, the Soviet-backed Lublin Committee was imposed over the London-based government-in-exile despite Yalta's promise of free elections. In Romania and Bulgaria, Soviet-backed coalition governments with dominant communist factions were installed. In Yugoslavia, Tito's communist partisans consolidated power. Hungary and Czechoslovakia initially retained more pluralistic governments but faced increasing Soviet pressure. This process — which Churchill in March 1946 would describe as an 'iron curtain' descending across Europe — laid the foundations of the Cold War division of the continent that would persist until 1989.

sources.name: Anne Applebaum, Iron Curtain: The Crushing of Eastern Europe, 1944–1956 (Doubleday, 2012)
Vojtech Mastny, Russia's Road to the Cold War (Columbia University Press, 1979)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through diplomatic records, declassified Soviet archives, and extensive historiography. The precise mechanisms of Soviet control varied by country.

events.title: Death of Franklin D. Roosevelt; Truman becomes President
events.description: On 12 April 1945, President Franklin D. Roosevelt died of a cerebral haemorrhage at Warm Springs, Georgia, just weeks before Germany's surrender. Roosevelt had served an unprecedented twelve years as president, leading the nation through the Great Depression and most of World War II. Vice President Harry S. Truman, who had been kept largely uninformed about major wartime decisions including the Manhattan Project, assumed the presidency. Truman would make several of the most consequential decisions of the century within his first months in office, including the use of atomic weapons against Japan, the acceptance of Soviet terms at Potsdam, and the initial framework for containment policy.
key_figures: Franklin D. Roosevelt (32nd President, died in office)

sources.name: David McCullough, Truman (Simon & Schuster, 1992)
Harry S. Truman, Memoirs, Volume I: Year of Decisions (Doubleday, 1955)

events.certainty_note: Among the most thoroughly documented presidential transitions in American history.

events.title: Vietnamese independence declared; First Indochina War begins
events.description: On 2 September 1945 — the same day Japan formally surrendered — Ho Chi Minh proclaimed the independence of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam in Hanoi, quoting from the American Declaration of Independence. The Viet Minh, the communist-led independence movement that had resisted Japanese occupation with OSS support, filled the power vacuum left by Japan's collapse in what is known as the August Revolution. However, the Potsdam agreement had assigned British forces to occupy southern Vietnam and Chinese Nationalist forces the north. France, determined to restore its colonial empire, began reasserting control. Negotiations between Ho Chi Minh and France broke down, leading to the outbreak of the First Indochina War in December 1946, a conflict that would culminate at Dien Bien Phu in 1954 and ultimately draw the United States into the Vietnam War.
key_figures: Ho Chi Minh (Viet Minh leader, declared independence)
Bảo Đại (last Vietnamese emperor, abdicated August 1945)
Archimedes Patti (OSS officer, liaised with Viet Minh)

sources.name: Vietnamese Declaration of Independence, 2 September 1945
Fredrik Logevall, Embers of War: The Fall of an Empire and the Making of America's Vietnam (Random House, 2012)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented. The text of the declaration and contemporary accounts survive. The August Revolution's internal dynamics are less fully documented due to the chaotic conditions.

events.title: Indonesian independence declared
events.description: On 17 August 1945, two days after Japan's surrender announcement, Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta proclaimed Indonesian independence in Jakarta, ending over three centuries of Dutch colonial rule. The declaration followed years of Japanese occupation during which Indonesian nationalists had been permitted to build political and military organisations. The Netherlands refused to recognise independence, and a four-year Indonesian National Revolution followed, involving armed resistance, diplomacy, and two major Dutch military offensives ('Police Actions'). International pressure, particularly from the United States and through the United Nations, eventually compelled the Netherlands to transfer sovereignty in December 1949. The Indonesian revolution became a landmark in the decolonisation of Asia.
key_figures: Mohammad Hatta (co-proclaimed independence, first vice president)

sources.name: Proclamation of Indonesian Independence, 17 August 1945
George McTurnan Kahin, Nationalism and Revolution in Indonesia (Cornell University Press, 1952)

events.certainty_note: The declaration is a preserved primary document. The subsequent revolution is well-documented through Dutch, Indonesian, and international diplomatic records.

events.title: Nuremberg trials begin
events.description: On 20 November 1945, the International Military Tribunal began proceedings against 22 major Nazi leaders at the Palace of Justice in Nuremberg. The tribunal, composed of judges from the United States, Soviet Union, United Kingdom, and France, tried defendants on charges of conspiracy to wage aggressive war, crimes against peace, war crimes, and crimes against humanity — the last being a newly codified category. The trial produced an enormous documentary record of Nazi atrocities and established foundational precedents in international criminal law: that individuals bear personal responsibility for crimes under international law regardless of state orders, and that 'following orders' is not an absolute defence. The proceedings concluded on 1 October 1946 with 12 death sentences.
key_figures: Hermann Göring (most senior defendant, committed suicide before execution)

sources.name: Trial of the Major War Criminals before the International Military Tribunal, Nuremberg, 14 November 1945 – 1 October 1946 (42 volumes, 'Blue Series')
Telford Taylor, The Anatomy of the Nuremberg Trials (Knopf, 1992)

events.certainty_note: The trial transcript, documentary evidence, and film footage are fully preserved and publicly accessible.

events.title: British Labour Party wins landslide; Attlee replaces Churchill
events.description: On 26 July 1945, the results of the British general election (held on 5 July, with counting delayed for overseas military ballots) produced a stunning Labour landslide: 393 seats to the Conservatives' 197. Clement Attlee replaced Winston Churchill as Prime Minister even as Churchill was attending the Potsdam Conference. The result reflected the British public's desire for post-war social reform rather than a return to pre-war conservatism, despite Churchill's wartime prestige. The Attlee government (1945–1951) would implement transformative domestic policies including the creation of the National Health Service, nationalisation of key industries, and the foundation of the modern welfare state, as well as overseeing the beginning of decolonisation in India and Palestine.
key_figures: Aneurin Bevan (Minister of Health, architect of the NHS)

sources.name: David Kynaston, Austerity Britain 1945–1951 (Bloomsbury, 2007)
Contemporary election results and press coverage, The Times and Manchester Guardian archives

events.certainty_note: Fully documented through official election records and extensive press coverage.

events.title: Arab League founded
events.description: On 22 March 1945, the League of Arab States (Arab League) was formally established with the signing of the Pact of the League of Arab States in Cairo by Egypt, Iraq, Jordan (then Transjordan), Lebanon, Saudi Arabia, Syria, and Yemen. The League aimed to coordinate political, economic, and cultural cooperation among Arab states and to represent Arab interests on the international stage. It reflected growing Arab nationalist aspirations, partly catalysed by British wartime promises and the anticipated end of French mandates in Syria and Lebanon. The League would play a significant role in opposing the creation of Israel and coordinating (with limited success) pan-Arab policies in subsequent decades.

sources.name: Pact of the League of Arab States, 22 March 1945
Bruce Maddy-Weitzman, The Crystallization of the Arab State System, 1945–1954 (Syracuse University Press, 1993)

events.certainty_note: The founding charter is a surviving primary document. The diplomatic process leading to its creation is well documented in British and Arab archives.

events.title: Dresden firebombed by Allied air forces
events.description: From 13–15 February 1945, British and American bomber forces conducted a massive air raid on Dresden, a major cultural centre in eastern Germany. British Bomber Command conducted two night raids using incendiary bombs that created a devastating firestorm, followed by USAAF daytime raids. The attack killed an estimated 22,700–25,000 people according to an independent German historical commission (2010), though post-war estimates had ranged wildly — Nazi propaganda at the time inflated figures to 200,000 or more. The bombing of Dresden became one of the most controversial Allied actions of the war, debated on grounds of military necessity (the city was a transportation hub but also a refugee centre) and proportionality. It remains a focal point in debates over the ethics of strategic area bombing.
key_figures: Carl Spaatz (USAAF Strategic Air Forces commander)

sources.name: Rolf-Dieter Müller (ed.), Der Bombenkrieg 1939–1945: Historische Kommission der Landeshauptstadt Dresden (commissioned historical report, 2010)
Frederick Taylor, Dresden: Tuesday, February 13, 1945 (HarperCollins, 2004)
RAF Bomber Command operational records, The National Archives (Kew)

events.certainty_note: The bombing is confirmed; casualty figures were definitively established by the Dresden Historical Commission (2010) at approximately 22,700–25,000, resolving decades of inflated estimates.

events.title: Bretton Woods institutions become operational
events.description: The International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (World Bank) formally came into existence on 27 December 1945, following ratification of the Articles of Agreement drafted at the Bretton Woods Conference in July 1944. These institutions established the post-war international economic order based on fixed exchange rates pegged to the U.S. dollar (itself convertible to gold at $35 per ounce), with the IMF providing short-term balance-of-payments assistance and the World Bank financing reconstruction and development. The Bretton Woods system reflected American economic dominance and aimed to prevent the competitive devaluations and trade wars that had deepened the Great Depression. It would govern international monetary relations until the Nixon Shock of 1971.
key_figures: Harry Dexter White (U.S. Treasury architect of the system)

sources.name: Articles of Agreement of the International Monetary Fund, 27 December 1945
Benn Steil, The Battle of Bretton Woods (Princeton University Press, 2013)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented through the Articles of Agreement and extensive diplomatic records from the Bretton Woods Conference.

events.title: Division of Korea at the 38th parallel
events.description: In August 1945, as Soviet forces rapidly advanced into northern Korea following their declaration of war on Japan, U.S. officials Dean Rusk and Charles Bonesteel hastily proposed the 38th parallel as a dividing line for accepting Japanese surrenders — the Soviets north, the Americans south. The Soviets, to American surprise, accepted this line despite having the military capacity to occupy the entire peninsula. This improvised military demarcation, made without Korean input, created the division that would lead to the establishment of two separate Korean states in 1948 and ultimately to the Korean War in 1950. The division was one of the most consequential snap decisions of the post-war era.
key_figures: Dean Rusk (U.S. War Department, proposed the line)
John R. Hodge (U.S. XXIV Corps commander in southern Korea)

sources.name: Dean Rusk, As I Saw It (W.W. Norton, 1990) — memoir account of proposing the 38th parallel
Bruce Cumings, The Origins of the Korean War, Vol. 1 (Princeton University Press, 1981)

events.certainty_note: Confirmed by Rusk's own account and military records. The precise circumstances of the decision are well-attested.

events.title: End of the Chinese wartime united front; civil war resumes
events.description: Japan's surrender in August 1945 ended the Second Sino-Japanese War but immediately reignited the struggle between Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government and Mao Zedong's Chinese Communist Party. Both sides raced to accept Japanese surrenders and seize territory — the Nationalists aided by American airlifts into cities, the Communists expanding from their rural base areas with captured Japanese weapons transferred by Soviet forces in Manchuria. The Chongqing Negotiations (28 August–10 October) between Mao and Chiang produced a nominal agreement on peaceful democratisation (the Double Tenth Agreement) that neither side intended to honour. The U.S. dispatched General George Marshall to mediate, but full-scale civil war erupted by mid-1946, culminating in Communist victory in 1949.
key_figures: Patrick Hurley (U.S. Ambassador, resigned in frustration)
George Marshall (dispatched as special envoy, December 1945)

sources.name: Odd Arne Westad, Decisive Encounters: The Chinese Civil War, 1946–1950 (Stanford University Press, 2003)
The China White Paper (U.S. State Department, 1949) — published documentary record of U.S.–China relations

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through American, Nationalist, and (after declassification) Communist archival sources.

events.title: Establishment of the Ethiopian-Eritrean question at the UN
events.description: With the defeat of Italy, the disposition of its former colonies — Libya, Eritrea, and Italian Somaliland — became a contentious issue in post-war diplomacy. British forces had administered Eritrea since 1941. Ethiopia under Emperor Haile Selassie, restored to his throne in 1941 after the Italian occupation, pressed claims for Eritrea based on historical ties and access to the sea. The question was eventually referred to the United Nations, beginning a protracted process that would lead to Eritrea's UN-mandated federation with Ethiopia in 1952 — a solution that satisfied neither Eritrean independence advocates nor Ethiopian annexationists and planted the seeds of a thirty-year independence war.

sources.name: Ruth Iyob, The Eritrean Struggle for Independence (Cambridge University Press, 1995)
British Foreign Office records on the disposal of Italian colonies, The National Archives (Kew)

events.certainty_note: The diplomatic process is documented in British, UN, and Ethiopian records. The political dynamics within Eritrea during this transitional period are less thoroughly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly held beliefs about 1945 are contested or complicated by modern scholarship: (1) The 'atomic diplomacy' thesis — that Truman used the bombs primarily to intimidate the Soviet Union rather than to end the war with Japan — remains debated. Historians like Gar Alperovitz have argued this case, while others like Robert Maddox have vigorously disputed it. The evidence does not support a single-factor explanation. (2) The belief that the atomic bombings alone ended the war is challenged by Tsuyoshi Hasegawa (Racing the Enemy, 2005), who argues that the Soviet declaration of war on 8 August was at least equally decisive in Japanese decision-making, as it destroyed hopes of Soviet-mediated peace terms. (3) The Dresden death toll was wildly inflated for decades — figures of 100,000–500,000 appeared in popular accounts and were cited by Kurt Vonnegut and David Irving. The 2010 Dresden Historical Commission established the figure at approximately 22,700–25,000. (4) The narrative of Yalta as a Western 'betrayal' of Eastern Europe, prominent in American conservative politics, oversimplifies the military realities: the Red Army already occupied most of the region, and Roosevelt's leverage was limited. (5) The extent of Hirohito's personal responsibility for the war and his role in the surrender decision remains debated; the post-war narrative of the Emperor as a passive constitutional monarch who heroically intervened for peace has been significantly complicated by scholars like Herbert Bix.
historiographic_note: 1945 is one of the most exhaustively documented years in human history. The surviving record is enormous: military archives from all major belligerents, diplomatic correspondence, tribunal transcripts, photographic and film documentation, and extensive memoirs. However, significant biases shape the record. The historiography was long dominated by Western (particularly American and British) perspectives; Soviet archives only became partially accessible after 1991, and Japanese wartime records were partially destroyed. Chinese Communist Party archives remain largely restricted. The voices of colonised peoples — Koreans, Vietnamese, Indonesians, Indians — asserting independence in 1945 were long marginalised in Western historiography but have received increasing scholarly attention since the 1980s. Civilian experiences, particularly those of women, refugees, and displaced populations, are underrepresented relative to military and diplomatic history. The Cold War shaped post-war historiography profoundly, with both Western and Soviet scholars framing 1945's events through ideological lenses that later scholarship has worked to correct.

graph_edges.note: Yalta followed from the Tehran Conference and the military situation in early 1945
Fall of Berlin was the culmination of the European war begun with Germany's invasion of Poland
Trinity test was the culmination of the Manhattan Project initiated after Fermi's sustained chain reaction
Atomic bombings were enabled by the successful Trinity test
Atomic bombings, combined with Soviet entry, precipitated Japanese surrender
Soviet occupation of northern Korea and the subsequent division led to the Korean War
Soviet domination of Eastern Europe directly prompted the Truman Doctrine and containment policy
Vietnamese declaration of independence and French resistance led to the First Indochina War
Indonesian declaration of independence led to the four-year Indonesian National Revolution
Nuremberg trials continued through 1946 and established precedents for international criminal law
Bretton Woods system governed international monetary relations until the Nixon Shock
Resumption of Chinese civil war in 1945 led to Communist victory and founding of the PRC in 1949
The UN was designed to succeed the failed League of Nations, incorporating lessons from its structural weaknesses
Holocaust revelations contributed directly to the Universal Declaration of Human Rights
Arab League founding shaped the coordinated Arab response to the creation of Israel
Labour landslide led to creation of the NHS and the modern British welfare state
Division at 38th parallel led directly to the establishment of rival Korean states and the Korean War
